questions.question_text: Which statement best describes particles of matter in particulate nature of matter?

options.text: Particles of matter have spaces between them, and the amount of space varies between states.
Matter is made of extremely small particles that have spaces and are in continuous motion.
Particles move continuously, and their average motion changes with temperature.
In a solid, particles are closely packed and mainly vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.explanation: Matter is made of extremely small particles that have spaces and are in continuous motion.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with particles of matter?

options.text: A drop of ink spreads faster in warm water than in cold water.
Air can be compressed more easily than a solid block.
The smell of perfume can spread through a room.
A metal spoon keeps its shape when placed on a table.

questions.explanation: The smell of perfume can spread through a room.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes particles of matter from a nearby idea?

options.text: A solid has a definite shape and volume because its particles are held in an organised arrangement.
Compressibility provides evidence of particle spacing rather than proving that matter is empty.
Temperature affects average particle motion; it does not create the particles.
The particle model explains observations without requiring particles to be visible directly.

questions.explanation: The particle model explains observations without requiring particles to be visible directly.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of particles of matter?

options.text: Use the model to explain diffusion, compression, and changes of state.
Compare how air and water respond when gently pressed in suitable syringes.
Compare diffusion under controlled temperature conditions.
Use particle spacing and motion to explain why a solid resists compression.

questions.explanation: Use the model to explain diffusion, compression, and changes of state.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about particles of matter?

options.text: Spaces between particles do not mean that a material has no mass.
Particles are not necessarily motionless just because a substance appears still.
A solid can have particle motion even when its shape remains fixed.
Solid particles are not completely without motion.

questions.explanation: Particles are not necessarily motionless just because a substance appears still.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining particles of matter. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: Particles move continuously, and their average motion changes with temperature.
Particles of matter have spaces between them, and the amount of space varies between states.
Matter is made of extremely small particles that have spaces and are in continuous motion.
In a solid, particles are closely packed and mainly vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.explanation: Matter is made of extremely small particles that have spaces and are in continuous motion.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which observation would provide useful evidence about particles of matter?

options.text: A metal spoon keeps its shape when placed on a table.
Air can be compressed more easily than a solid block.
A drop of ink spreads faster in warm water than in cold water.
The smell of perfume can spread through a room.

questions.explanation: The smell of perfume can spread through a room.

options.text: Use the model to explain diffusion, compression, and changes of state.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes spaces between particles in particulate nature of matter?

options.text: In a liquid, particles remain close but can move past one another, so the liquid flows.
Particles move continuously, and their average motion changes with temperature.
In a solid, particles are closely packed and mainly vibrate around fixed positions.
Particles of matter have spaces between them, and the amount of space varies between states.

questions.explanation: Particles of matter have spaces between them, and the amount of space varies between states.

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with spaces between particles?

options.text: Air can be compressed more easily than a solid block.
A drop of ink spreads faster in warm water than in cold water.
A metal spoon keeps its shape when placed on a table.
Water takes the shape of its container while keeping nearly the same volume.

questions.explanation: Air can be compressed more easily than a solid block.

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes spaces between particles from a nearby idea?

options.text: Temperature affects average particle motion; it does not create the particles.

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of spaces between particles?

options.text: Use particle spacing and motion to explain why a solid resists compression.
Compare diffusion under controlled temperature conditions.
Compare how air and water respond when gently pressed in suitable syringes.
Explain pouring using the ability of liquid particles to move past one another.

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about spaces between particles?

options.text: A liquid does not take up no space just because it takes the container's shape.
A solid can have particle motion even when its shape remains fixed.
Solid particles are not completely without motion.
Spaces between particles do not mean that a material has no mass.

questions.explanation: Spaces between particles do not mean that a material has no mass.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes motion of particles in particulate nature of matter?

options.text: In a solid, particles are closely packed and mainly vibrate around fixed positions.
Particles move continuously, and their average motion changes with temperature.
In a liquid, particles remain close but can move past one another, so the liquid flows.
In a gas, particles are far apart and move freely, so the gas fills its container.

questions.explanation: Particles move continuously, and their average motion changes with temperature.

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with motion of particles?

options.text: Water takes the shape of its container while keeping nearly the same volume.
A metal spoon keeps its shape when placed on a table.
A drop of ink spreads faster in warm water than in cold water.
Air spreads through an entire room.

questions.explanation: A drop of ink spreads faster in warm water than in cold water.

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes motion of particles from a nearby idea?

options.text: A gas has neither a fixed shape nor a fixed volume under ordinary conditions.
A solid has a definite shape and volume because its particles are held in an organised arrangement.
A liquid has a definite volume but no fixed shape of its own.
Temperature affects average particle motion; it does not create the particles.

questions.explanation: Temperature affects average particle motion; it does not create the particles.

options.text: Compare diffusion under controlled temperature conditions.

Use a sealed syringe or balloon to observe that a gas occupies space and can be compressed.

options.text: Solid particles are not completely without motion.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes solid state in particulate nature of matter?

options.text: Diffusion is the spreading of particles from a region of higher concentration to lower concentration due to random motion.
In a liquid, particles remain close but can move past one another, so the liquid flows.
In a gas, particles are far apart and move freely, so the gas fills its container.
In a solid, particles are closely packed and mainly vibrate around fixed positions.

questions.explanation: In a solid, particles are closely packed and mainly vibrate around fixed positions.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with solid state?

options.text: A metal spoon keeps its shape when placed on a table.
Water takes the shape of its container while keeping nearly the same volume.
Air spreads through an entire room.
A scent spreads from a perfumed area into nearby air.

questions.explanation: A metal spoon keeps its shape when placed on a table.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes solid state from a nearby idea?

options.text: A liquid has a definite volume but no fixed shape of its own.
A solid has a definite shape and volume because its particles are held in an organised arrangement.
A gas has neither a fixed shape nor a fixed volume under ordinary conditions.
Diffusion is a particle process and does not require stirring in every situation.

questions.explanation: A solid has a definite shape and volume because its particles are held in an organised arrangement.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of solid state?

options.text: Use a sealed syringe or balloon to observe that a gas occupies space and can be compressed.
Explain pouring using the ability of liquid particles to move past one another.
Use particle spacing and motion to explain why a solid resists compression.
Compare diffusion rates while keeping the amount of substance and container size controlled.

questions.explanation: Use particle spacing and motion to explain why a solid resists compression.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about solid state?

options.text: Diffusion does not mean that particles all travel in one straight direction.
A liquid does not take up no space just because it takes the container's shape.
A gas is not weightless merely because it is hard to see.
Solid particles are not completely without motion.

questions.explanation: Solid particles are not completely without motion.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes liquid state in particulate nature of matter?

In a liquid, particles remain close but can move past one another, so the liquid flows.
Diffusion is the spreading of particles from a region of higher concentration to lower concentration due to random motion.

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with gaseous state?

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes gaseous state from a nearby idea?

options.text: Diffusion is a particle process and does not require stirring in every situation.

options.text: Predict how a substance may expand or change state when its temperature changes.

Use a sealed syringe or balloon to observe that a gas occupies space and can be compressed.

A scent spreads from a perfumed area into nearby air.

questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes diffusion from a nearby idea?

options.text: The model is judged by how well it explains several observations together.
Temperature is related to average particle motion, not simply to how much matter is present.
A physical change of state does not create a new substance.
Diffusion is a particle process and does not require stirring in every situation.

questions.explanation: Diffusion is a particle process and does not require stirring in every situation.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of diffusion?

options.text: Compare diffusion rates while keeping the amount of substance and container size controlled.
Predict how a substance may expand or change state when its temperature changes.
Track heating and cooling to identify melting, freezing, evaporation, and condensation.
Use more than one observation when evaluating a particle-based explanation.

questions.explanation: Compare diffusion rates while keeping the amount of substance and container size controlled.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about diffusion?

options.text: A larger sample is not necessarily at a higher temperature.
Diffusion does not mean that particles all travel in one straight direction.
Evaporation can occur at the surface below the boiling point.
One observation alone does not show that every detail of a model is proven.

questions.explanation: Diffusion does not mean that particles all travel in one straight direction.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: A learner is explaining diffusion. Which description is most accurate?

options.text: Changes of state occur when particle energy and arrangement change while the substance remains the same material.
Heating usually increases average particle motion, while cooling usually decreases it.
Diffusion is the spreading of particles from a region of higher concentration to lower concentration due to random motion.
Observable changes such as diffusion, compression, and expansion support the particle model of matter.

questions.explanation: Diffusion is the spreading of particles from a region of higher concentration to lower concentration due to random motion.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which observation would provide useful evidence about diffusion?

options.text: A sealed balloon changes size when warmed, showing a change in gas behaviour.
Heating a gas can make it expand if pressure is allowed to remain suitable.
Ice melts to liquid water and water vapour can condense back to liquid water.
A scent spreads from a perfumed area into nearby air.

questions.explanation: A scent spreads from a perfumed area into nearby air.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which action is the most appropriate use of the idea of diffusion?

options.text: Compare diffusion rates while keeping the amount of substance and container size controlled.
Predict how a substance may expand or change state when its temperature changes.
Track heating and cooling to identify melting, freezing, evaporation, and condensation.
Use more than one observation when evaluating a particle-based explanation.

questions.explanation: Compare diffusion rates while keeping the amount of substance and container size controlled.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes temperature and particles in particulate nature of matter?

options.text: Matter is made of extremely small particles that have spaces and are in continuous motion.
Changes of state occur when particle energy and arrangement change while the substance remains the same material.
Observable changes such as diffusion, compression, and expansion support the particle model of matter.
Heating usually increases average particle motion, while cooling usually decreases it.

questions.explanation: Heating usually increases average particle motion, while cooling usually decreases it.

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with temperature and particles?

options.text: Heating a gas can make it expand if pressure is allowed to remain suitable.
Ice melts to liquid water and water vapour can condense back to liquid water.
A sealed balloon changes size when warmed, showing a change in gas behaviour.
The smell of perfume can spread through a room.

questions.explanation: Heating a gas can make it expand if pressure is allowed to remain suitable.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes changes of state in particulate nature of matter?

options.text: Observable changes such as diffusion, compression, and expansion support the particle model of matter.
Changes of state occur when particle energy and arrangement change while the substance remains the same material.
Matter is made of extremely small particles that have spaces and are in continuous motion.
Particles of matter have spaces between them, and the amount of space varies between states.

questions.explanation: Changes of state occur when particle energy and arrangement change while the substance remains the same material.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with changes of state?

options.text: The smell of perfume can spread through a room.
A sealed balloon changes size when warmed, showing a change in gas behaviour.
Ice melts to liquid water and water vapour can condense back to liquid water.
Air can be compressed more easily than a solid block.

questions.explanation: Ice melts to liquid water and water vapour can condense back to liquid water.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes changes of state from a nearby idea?

options.text: Compressibility provides evidence of particle spacing rather than proving that matter is empty.
The model is judged by how well it explains several observations together.
The particle model explains observations without requiring particles to be visible directly.
A physical change of state does not create a new substance.

questions.explanation: A physical change of state does not create a new substance.

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of changes of state?

options.text: Track heating and cooling to identify melting, freezing, evaporation, and condensation.
Use more than one observation when evaluating a particle-based explanation.
Use the model to explain diffusion, compression, and changes of state.
Compare how air and water respond when gently pressed in suitable syringes.

questions.explanation: Track heating and cooling to identify melting, freezing, evaporation, and condensation.

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about changes of state?

options.text: One observation alone does not show that every detail of a model is proven.
Evaporation can occur at the surface below the boiling point.
Particles are not necessarily motionless just because a substance appears still.
Spaces between particles do not mean that a material has no mass.

questions.question_text: Which statement best describes evidence for the particle model in particulate nature of matter?

options.text: Particles move continuously, and their average motion changes with temperature.
Matter is made of extremely small particles that have spaces and are in continuous motion.
Particles of matter have spaces between them, and the amount of space varies between states.
Observable changes such as diffusion, compression, and expansion support the particle model of matter.

questions.explanation: Observable changes such as diffusion, compression, and expansion support the particle model of matter.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which example is most directly associated with evidence for the particle model?

options.text: A sealed balloon changes size when warmed, showing a change in gas behaviour.
The smell of perfume can spread through a room.
Air can be compressed more easily than a solid block.
A drop of ink spreads faster in warm water than in cold water.

questions.explanation: A sealed balloon changes size when warmed, showing a change in gas behaviour.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement best distinguishes evidence for the particle model from a nearby idea?

options.text: The particle model explains observations without requiring particles to be visible directly.
The model is judged by how well it explains several observations together.
Compressibility provides evidence of particle spacing rather than proving that matter is empty.
Temperature affects average particle motion; it does not create the particles.

questions.explanation: The model is judged by how well it explains several observations together.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which situation best applies the idea of evidence for the particle model?

options.text: Compare how air and water respond when gently pressed in suitable syringes.
Use the model to explain diffusion, compression, and changes of state.
Use more than one observation when evaluating a particle-based explanation.
Compare diffusion under controlled temperature conditions.

questions.explanation: Use more than one observation when evaluating a particle-based explanation.
questions.source_locator: Chapter 7; original question derived from the chapter concept manifest

questions.question_text: Which statement corrects a common misunderstanding about evidence for the particle model?

options.text: A solid can have particle motion even when its shape remains fixed.
Particles are not necessarily motionless just because a substance appears still.
Spaces between particles do not mean that a material has no mass.
One observation alone does not show that every detail of a model is proven.

questions.explanation: One observation alone does not show that every detail of a model is proven.

Particles move continuously, and their average motion changes with temperature.
